reason: A covers all four key points (existential risk, capturing users directly, diversifying acquisition channels, and fast-detect/fast-repair engineering) with concrete, correct, well-organized actions (remote config vs. Chrome Web Store code-policy nuance, stable ARIA/`ytd-*` selectors, synthetic monitoring, layered fallbacks). B is concise and correct but omits explicit acquisition-channel diversification — it uses the YouTube Data API only as a functional fallback, not as a strategic diversification move — leaving a real gap on one of the most important points. A's added length is substantive rather than padding, so it doesn't run afoul of the length-isn't-merit principle.
